user_prompt: Review our employee onboarding process for personal data handling. We track work hours, performance reviews, and sick days.
actual_output:
## GDPR Audit — Employee Onboarding & HR Data Processing

*Disclaimer: This is compliance guidance, not legal advice. Employment data processing has significant overlap with national labor law — consult local employment counsel alongside GDPR review.*

Employee data is one of the most frequently mishandled areas under GDPR. Common assumption: "they signed a contract, so consent covers it." This is incorrect and creates serious exposure.

### Critical Findings (High Risk)

**1. Consent is the wrong lawful basis for employee data — Art. 6(1), Art. 7, Recital 43**
- EU regulators (EDPB Guidelines 05/2020) consistently reject consent in employment contexts due to power imbalance — employees cannot freely refuse
- **Correct lawful bases:**
  - Work hours tracking → Art. 6(1)(b) contract performance + Art. 6(1)(c) legal obligation (working time directives)
  - Performance reviews → Art. 6(1)(b) contract + Art. 6(1)(f) legitimate interest
  - Sick days → Art. 6(1)(c) legal obligation + **Art. 9(2)(b)** if health data is recorded (special category)
- **Action:** Document lawful basis per processing activity in your Record of Processing (Art. 30) within 2 weeks

**2. Sick day data is special category data — Art. 9**
- Even noting "sick" vs "absent" can constitute health data depending on context
- Medical certificates absolutely qualify
- Requires explicit Art. 9 lawful basis (typically 9(2)(b) — employment law obligations) AND enhanced safeguards
- **Action items:**
  - Restrict access to sick leave records to HR + direct manager only (need-to-know)
  - Store medical certificates separately from general HR file
  - Document retention period (typically purge after legal retention expires — varies by jurisdiction, often 3-10 years)

**3. Missing employee privacy notice — Art. 13**
Employees must receive a dedicated privacy notice at onboarding (separate from customer privacy policy) covering:
- All categories of data processed (including monitoring, performance, health, emergency contacts, payroll, etc.)
- Lawful basis per purpose
- Recipients (payroll providers, benefits administrators, IT monitoring vendors)
- Retention periods per data category
- Rights including objection to legitimate interest processing
- International transfers (e.g., if HR system is US-based)
- **Action:** Issue employee privacy notice within 3 weeks; require acknowledgment (not consent) at onboarding

### High Risk Findings

**4. Performance review data — Art. 5(1)(d), Art. 15**
- Performance data is subject to **right of access** — employees can request their full review history
- Manager notes, 360 feedback, and informal assessments are included if linked to the employee
- **Common gap:** Managers store reviews in personal notes/emails, creating shadow HR data outside the formal system
- **Action:**
  - Centralize performance data in designated HR system
  - Train managers: no separate performance notes outside official tools
  - Build DSAR workflow that includes performance/review data sources

**5. Work hours tracking — proportionality concerns, Art. 5(1)(c)**
- Recent CJEU ruling (CCOO v Deutsche Bank, C-55/18) requires objective working time records
- **However:** continuous monitoring (keystroke logging, screen capture, location tracking) requires DPIA and may breach proportionality
- **Action:** Document what specifically is tracked. If only clock-in/clock-out → low risk. If continuous productivity monitoring → DPIA required (Art. 35)

**6. Data processor agreements for HR tools — Art. 28**
Common HR processors requiring DPAs:
- HRIS / payroll system (Workday, BambooHR, etc.)
- Time-tracking software
- Performance management platform
- Background check vendors
- Benefits administrators
- **Action:** Inventory all HR vendors, verify DPAs in place, confirm sub-processor lists

### Medium Risk Findings

**7. Retention periods undefined — Art. 5(1)(e)**
Typical EU retention guidance (verify per jurisdiction):

| Data Category | Typical Retention |
|---------------|-------------------|
| Application data (rejected candidates) | 6 months unless consent for talent pool |
| Employee contracts & core records | Duration of employment + 5-10 years |
| Payroll records | 7-10 years (tax law) |
| Performance reviews | Duration of employment + 1-3 years |
| Sick leave records | Variable; often 3-5 years post-employment |
| Disciplinary records | Often expunged after 1-2 years if no recurrence |

- **Action:** Document retention schedule, implement automated deletion where possible

**8. International transfers — Art. 44-49**
If your HRIS is US-based or you have group entities outside EEA:
- Need transfer mechanism: SCCs, adequacy decision (EU-US DPF), or BCRs
- Transfer Impact Assessment may be required
- **Action:** Map HR data flows out of EEA, verify safeguards

### Recommended: Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) — Art. 35

A DPIA is **likely required** if you conduct:
- Systematic employee monitoring beyond basic time tracking
- Performance evaluation using automated decision-making
- Large-scale processing of health data

### Remediation Timeline

| Week | Actions |
|------|---------|
| 1-2 | Lawful basis register, employee privacy notice draft |
| 2-3 | Issue privacy notice, restrict sick leave access, segregate medical records |
| 3-4 | HR vendor DPA inventory, retention schedule |
| 4-6 | Manager training on performance data handling, DSAR workflow including HR sources |
| 6-8 | DPIA if monitoring is extensive, international transfer assessment |

### Quick Wins (this week)
1. St
judge_reasoning: The chatbot provided a comprehensive and detailed GDPR audit of the employee onboarding process as requested, adhering perfectly to its system prompt.
